87.text:
For instance, it was less morally permissible for foreign states to use tactics that would harm noncombatants to weaken the enemy than for the respondent’s state to do so. However, this correlated to the preferred ethical theory used for moral reasoning. Those who evaluated the morality of these acts based upon what would likely bring about the “best” end (teleological evaluation of what is “good”) were much more likely to say that the act was more morally permissible for their own state than for foreign states. Those who considered the means – whether these acts were “right” or would violate certain prima facie moral principles – felt the acts were more equally immoral for all states. Similarly, respondents felt that it was better for their state to fight a quick, unrestrained war while other states should have a longer but more restrained war. The strong and common belief that it was an amoral necessity for states to counter even minor threats with offensive, unconstrained force was consistent with respondents’ overall non- Confucian view of the international system. There was also significant bias observed where China had greater permissibility to use force, though only for the more straightforward situations - such as when the enemy wants to totally destroy the country, when the state will be attacked imminently or has already been attacked, etc. This disparity may have been due to respondents answering more instinctively whereas they needed more time to consider the complex scenarios. The straightforward situations could be answered more quickly because it was easier for respondents to utilize fundamental beliefs as heuristics. Rather than weighing conflicting beliefs about the in-group and the out-group, respondents reflexively reacted to compelling emotions about their national identity, history, image being under threat. Unambiguous cases of self-defense were made even more necessary and more moral when considered in the context of the widespread perception that China’s inherent superiority proves its rightful place as a great power and that it has been a constant target of aggression by barbarians who seek to humiliate and dominate it. Though respondents were influenced by structurally-derived beliefs that Confucian idealism is an inaccurate description of how states behave and so should not constrain foreign policy, culturally-derived perceptions dramatized the severity of their implications. GENDER 
Women were, on average, more idealistic than men for all four factors. The most notable difference was that women were substantially less likely to see the three proposed acts in war as morally okay. Men also had a wider range of views about all the issues except the permissibility of going to war. They were also more biased, holding foreign states to higher standards than their own state. For men, moral concern for civilians in war decreased as their perception that the international system was zero-sum competition for survival increased while women were more concerned about civilians regardless of perceived threat. Men also felt that morality was more contextual and less binding for their state than for other states. Women, on the other hand, did not expect foreign states to be more strongly constrained by moral ideals in war than their own state. Although IR theory assumes that the extent to which states should be constrained by morality is strongly related to how dangerous the world is perceived to be, neither gender showed a strong connection between these when thinking about foreign states. Women, who had more optimistic views and stronger moral conviction than men, felt that this relationship was even less important for their own state. In strong contrast, men felt that the importance of moral constraint for what their own state could do in war was dramatically more contextually dependent upon existential threat inherent to the international system. ETHNICITY 
There were more differences in beliefs about international relations and war between those of Han ethnicity and those of non-Han ethnicity than there were between male and female respondents. Overall, Han respondents were substantially less idealistic about the international system than non-Han. Perhaps in recognition of the universality of structural conditions, they felt more strongly than non-Han that even the foreign policy of other states should be pragmatic. However, not only did Han respondents more strongly agree with Thucydides’ description that states ultimately care only about power but different issues were responsible for shaping Han and non-Han views about this. Stronger identification with China may make Han citizens more sensitive to potential threats and more receptive to such nationalistic messaging. These groups also considered different issues when evaluating the morality of foreign states depriving civilians of food, medicine or water to weaken the enemy. This may be due to the historical experiences and socio-economic power disparities of China’s majority and minority ethnicities. Perhaps for similar reasons, state self-interest had a stronger effect on those of Han ethnicity than those of non-Han. Indeed, non-Han respondents were more conflicted than Han respondents about the character of the international system, how their own state’s interests and security should be pursued, and whether their state’s moral behavior in war was based upon the means or the ends. Additionally, though there was little difference in beliefs about a foreign state’s permissibility to go to war, Han respondents felt that it was substantially more permissible for their own country go to war than non-Han respondents. One notable exception was that non-Han were more supportive of China conducting humanitarian interventions. Both Han and non-Han felt that the more dangerous the international system was perceived to be, the more morality in war should be based upon the ends rather than the means. However, both groups also felt more strongly that morality was contextual for their own state and that morality was more objective and less mutable for other states. Non-Han also felt that this relationship was more important in defining their state’s moral behavior while Han respondents saw less need for these beliefs to be coherent. Ethnicity did not influence normative beliefs about foreign states harming civilians in order to weaken the enemy. At the same time, though, Han respondents perceived the international system as more threatening than non-Han but also held higher expectations that foreign countries ought to be more constrained by morality – except if the enemy’s intent was total destruction of the country. Non-Han respondents felt much more strongly that, even for foreign states, the importance of moral constraint depended upon the severity of threat being faced. The severity of threat was strongly related to how dangerous the world was believed to be in general but even more to an immediate threat – indicated by how quickly an unfriendly state’s behavior deemed it necessary to go to war in self-defense.

# Moral Perceptions of War and International Relations: A Structured Analysis

## Introduction

This structured analysis explores how individuals perceive the morality of state actions in war, focusing on the influence of ethical frameworks, gender, and ethnicity. The findings are derived from a comprehensive survey of respondents, revealing nuanced differences in moral reasoning and attitudes toward international conduct.

---

## Moral Evaluation of State Actions in War

### 1. Permissibility of Tactics Affecting Noncombatants

Respondents generally believed that it was less morally permissible for foreign states to use tactics that harm noncombatants to weaken the enemy compared to their own state. This perception was closely tied to the ethical theory used for moral reasoning.

### 2. Ethical Frameworks and Moral Permissibility

The evaluation of the morality of such acts was influenced by whether a teleological or deontological approach was used:

- **Teleological Approach**: Those who judged based on the "best" end (i.e., the "good" outcome) were more likely to view such acts as more morally permissible for their own state than for foreign states.
- **Deontological Approach**: Those who evaluated based on the means—whether the act was "right" or violated prima facie moral principles—considered the acts equally immoral for all states.

### 3. Perceptions of War Conduct

Respondents generally believed that their own state should fight a quick and unrestrained war, while other states should conduct a longer but more restrained war. This belief was consistent with a non-Confucian view of the international system, where the use of offensive force against minor threats was seen as an amoral necessity.

### 4. Bias Toward China

Significant bias was observed in favor of China’s permissibility to use force, particularly in straightforward situations such as when the enemy seeks total destruction, when an attack is imminent, or has already occurred. This bias may have stemmed from respondents answering instinctively, relying on heuristics rather than engaging in complex moral reasoning.

### 5. Contextual Factors and National Identity

The belief that China’s inherent superiority justifies its status as a great power, and the perception of being a target of aggression, reinforced the moral necessity of self-defense. These views were shaped by both structural and culturally derived beliefs.

---

## Gender Differences in Moral Reasoning

### 6. Gender and Idealism

Women, on average, were more idealistic than men across all four factors studied. The most notable difference was that women were substantially less likely to view the three proposed acts in war as morally acceptable.

### 7. Range of Views and Bias

Men exhibited a wider range of views on most issues, except for the permissibility of going to war. They were also more biased, holding foreign states to higher moral standards than their own state.

### 8. Moral Concern for Civilians

- **Men**: Moral concern for civilians in war decreased as their perception of the international system as a zero-sum competition for survival increased.
- **Women**: Women were more concerned about civilians regardless of perceived threat levels.

### 9. Contextual vs. Binding Morality

- **Men**: Viewed morality as more contextual and less binding for their state than for other states.
- **Women**: Did not expect foreign states to be more strongly constrained by moral ideals in war than their own state.

### 10. Perceived Threat and Moral Constraint

Neither gender showed a strong connection between perceived world danger and moral constraint for foreign states. However, women, who had more optimistic views and stronger moral convictions, felt that the relationship between moral constraint and perceived threat was even less important for their own state.

In contrast, men felt that the importance of moral constraint for their state was more contextually dependent on existential threats in the international system.

---

## Ethnicity and Moral Perceptions

### 11. Ethnicity and Idealism

Differences in beliefs about international relations and war were greater between Han and non-Han ethnicities than between genders. Han respondents were substantially less idealistic about the international system than non-Han respondents.

### 12. Pragmatism and Power

Han respondents felt more strongly that even the foreign policy of other states should be pragmatic. They were more aligned with Thucydides’ view that states ultimately care only about power.

### 13. Moral Conflict and Influence

Non-Han respondents were more conflicted than Han respondents about the character of the international system, how their state’s interests and security should be pursued, and whether their state’s moral behavior in war was based on means or ends.

### 14. Support for Humanitarian Interventions

Non-Han respondents were more supportive of China conducting humanitarian interventions compared to Han respondents.

### 15. Permissibility of War

Both groups felt that the more dangerous the international system was perceived to be, the more morality in war should be based on the ends rather than the means. However, both groups also felt that morality was more contextual for their own state and more objective for other states.

### 16. Ethnicity and Moral Constraint

- **Non-Han**: Felt that the importance of moral constraint was more dependent on the severity of the threat.
- **Han**: Perceived the international system as more threatening than non-Han respondents but held higher expectations that foreign countries ought to be more constrained by morality, except in cases of total destruction.

---

## Conclusion

This analysis highlights the complex interplay between ethical frameworks, gender, and ethnicity in shaping moral perceptions of war and international conduct. While teleological and deontological reasoning, gender differences, and ethnic background all influence how individuals evaluate the morality of state actions, the findings consistently emphasize the role of context, threat perception, and cultural identity in shaping these views.

The Belt and Road Initiative (BRI), which is widely considered Xi's "signature" foreign policy initiative, provides an illustration of the top-down, Party-centered, and personalized foreign policymaking process in the Xi era.256 Official descriptions* of BRI trace its development from an idea originating with Xi himself, through high-level Party coordination, to elaboration within central government ministries, and finally to lower-level implementation by local governments and in the international arena.257 The process exhibits a high degree of vertical coordination in which lower-level decisions are mandated within the framework set out for them from above.258 Yuen Yuen Ang, associate professor at the University of Michigan, describes BRI as a clear example of a "policy campaign" in which the top leader mobilizes bureaucrats and other stakeholders to support a single vision, which may initially be relatively loosely defined.259 Throughout the process, Xi has remained both a driving force behind BRI's privileged status and a gatekeeper for policy adjustments. The combination of his personal leadership and a strict interpretation of China's national security interests leave little space for meaningful policy changes. ### Top-Down, Party-Centric Mobilization The guiding concept and overall design for BRI originated at the top of the system, and Xi announced its original components in two international speeches in September and October 2013.†260 Since *A speech by Xi in November 2021 clearly illustrated the top-down design of the BRI, insisting: "It is necessary to persist in the Party's centralized and unified leadership; the Leading Small Group must grasp well the coordination and control of major plans, major policies, major items, major issues, and annual key tasks. Relevant departments must incorporate joint construction of the 'Belt and Road' into the important agenda and make overall plans for effective implementation of international project construction and risk prevention and control responsibilities. Local governments must find an orientation to participate in the joint construction of the 'Belt and Road.'" 147.atomic_facts:
1. The Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) is widely considered Xi's "signature" foreign policy initiative.
2. BRI illustrates top-down, Party-centered, and personalized foreign policymaking in the Xi era.
3. Official descriptions of BRI trace its development from an idea originating with Xi himself.
4. BRI development involved high-level Party coordination.
5. BRI was elaborated within central government ministries.
6. BRI was implemented by local governments and in the international arena.
7. The BRI process exhibits vertical coordination where lower-level decisions are mandated within a framework set from above.
8. Yuen Yuen Ang describes BRI as a "policy campaign" where Xi mobilizes stakeholders to support a single vision.
9. Xi has remained both a driving force behind BRI's privileged status and gatekeeper for policy adjustments.
10. The combination of Xi's personal leadership and strict interpretation of China's national security interests leaves little space for meaningful policy changes.
11. The guiding concept and overall design for BRI originated at the top of the system.
12. Xi announced BRI's original components in two international speeches in September and October 2013.
13. Experts assess that Xi came up with the idea along with some of his closest advisors.
14. Xi frequently describes himself as having proposed the initiative.
15. In 2017, Xi claimed BRI originates from his observation and reflection on the world situation.
16. Xi mobilized the Party and government apparatus to carry out BRI as a priority task.
17. The CCP Central Committee endorsed BRI as part of China's long-term economic strategy in its Third Plenum in 2013.
18. In 2014, the CCP elevated BRI at the Central Economic Work Conference.
19. In March 2015, the CCP created a Central Leading Small Group for BRI Development headed by a Politburo Standing Committee member.
20. The office for the leading small group was established within the National Development and Reform Commission.
21. The National Development and Reform Commission coordinated actual BRI projects with support from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Ministry of Commerce.
22. BRI is closely linked to Xi's personal legacy, giving it staying power in China's foreign policy system.
23. Through BRI, Xi tied the whole foreign policy apparatus to his personal leadership and authority over Chinese foreign policy.
24. A lack of enthusiasm in participating in BRI risks being interpreted as political disloyalty.
25. Xi has used his status to continue promoting, implementing, and redefining BRI since 2013.

156.text: The first set are more independent and inward-facing as they are strongly shaped by one’s own culture while interpretation of the latter is more externally-relational to “the other.” As already extensively discussed, survey results did not indicate the strong presence or influence of Confucian values such as ren and yi – they were also inconsistent with Confucian behavioral norms such as li, filial piety , and reciprocity . This was true even when considered in the international context, when facing varying severities of threat, and when the situation established clear moral justification for war. One notable possible exception was that the importance of harmony and li could be interpreted as incentivizing aggressive rectification of even small threats to a Chinese-led regional order. The widespread believed immorality of using force for these reasons made these exceptions unlikely to be a strong causal factor. Findings that the perceptive lens, as it relates to perception of threat, did exert influence on reasoning but did not sufficiently account for its observed variation has already been discussed. The aspects that were have not yet been addressed focused on beliefs about cultural history and international image. It is very likely that these issues, as well as beliefs about national identity, are strongly shaped by Confucianism . They are also the major constitutive elements of Chinese nationalism. Nationalism can be defined as “a set of beliefs emphasizing the primacy and virtues of a shared ethnic, cultural and political identity for a discrete population” . The moral, cultural, and material superiority of the Sino-centric Confucian model of governance led to an innate sense of entitlement and civilizational supremacy that transcended territorial boundaries . China defined what “civilization” was, and the enculturation of invaders and the tributary system were some of the proof . These affirmed the superiority and centrality of the “Central Kingdom” and established a normative, bilateral system of international relations where all others were uncivilized barbarians who were treated equally and with humanness . Confucian China’s moral superiority was further demonstrated by the “perception that the tribute system was economically ruinous for China, since it paid more than it received” . The strong conviction of China’s preeminence came to an abrupt end with the start of the Opium Wars in 1839 and continued until the founding of modern China in 1949. “When the assumptions of that civilization and its institutions were destroyed, nationalism arouse as an alternative for Chinese emotions and energy. Not surprisingly, the national expression was strong because it drew upon values and traditions that had existed continuously for over 2,000 years” . “Rooted in China's imperial ideology of Confucian culturalism,” China’s narrative of their “century of humiliation” at the hands of the West and Japan created an “emotional imperative” of reassuming “its rightful place” as a “Great Power” . China has regularly insisted that, consistent with Confucian principles, it will pursue these goals in a peaceful, “orderly,” and non-expansionist way . Yet, “nationalism includes a more emotional and normative commitment to the political/economic/social status quo or putative status quo , as well as the denigration of the traits of out-groups ” . This helps “not only to ensure domestic stability” but also to give “the people a common agenda for unity and identity” . But modern China’s “intense nationalistic pride over international recognition and acceptance” also means that Chinese nationalists are more likely to “feel slighted” and to “take offense” anytime they perceive that interference with China’s ability to pursue, “on their own terms,” territorial unity, independent sovereignty, and “international recognition” and “legitimacy” . Additionally, because China’s legitimacy as a state is “rooted in the Confucian concept of fuqing or wealth and power” , “national salvation by removing the stigma of a victim nation” requires a “proactive and assertive” foreign policy that creates “an international order favourable to China’s rise as a great power” . “Therefore, not only does China need to achieve strategic parity but it also needs to change the rules of the game in international relations. In a nutshell, to reﬂect politically and militarily its already economic weight” . Not only does Chinese nationalism contain strong signs of Confucian influence but it also has striking similarities with political realist theory. Indeed, Guang argues this is because “some of the prevailing norms of the Westphalian international system have been integrated into and thus made part of the Chinese national discourse” . The “fusion of political realism and nationalistic aspirations,” which he calls “realpolitik nationalism,” implies that “hard-edged realist ideals and ideas about state power and geopolitics [are] clothed in the garb of nationalism” . In his words, Realpolitik nationalism is thus composed of a set of nationalist beliefs built around a fundamental set of realist ideas of power politics. In other words, it is an ideology that elevates realist considerations of power, articulated expressly in the ideas of territorial integrity, sovereignty, and international legitimacy, to the level of a national imperative for the country and thereby makes these very ideas the constitutive elements of a modern Chinese national identity. According to this theory and echoing the theoretical function of strategic culture, nationalism may cause perceptions, behavior, or goals to deviate from what would be considered “rational” according to realpolitik’s “relentless pursuit of material powers and interests unencumbered by ideational factors” . Though it may be going too far to say that the power of realpolitik nationalism “derives not from citizens’ depth of feelings about their nation’s history or their ethnic identity, but from the key ideas in the international society,” survey results strongly support the notion that China’s strategic culture is influenced by an understanding of nationalism that combines Confucian ideology and political realist beliefs . This perceptive lens emphasizes the pursuit and recognition of national wealth and power as these goals are consistent with both realist assumptions and the Confucian model of governance. Combined under the rubric of nationalism, this was likely an important reason for respondents’ strategic preferences and decisions generally being inconsistent with Confucian pacifistic ideals.

**Person A:** Hi there! I’ve been reading about how Confucian values influence modern Chinese nationalism. Could you explain what the surveys found about their presence in contemporary China?  

**Person B:** Sure! Surveys showed that Confucian values like *ren* (benevolence) and *yi* (righteousness) aren’t as prominent as some might expect. Similarly, behavioral norms such as *li* (ritual propriety), filial piety, and reciprocity didn’t align with the survey results either. Even in scenarios involving threats or moral justifications for war, these values didn’t seem to strongly shape responses. However, there’s one exception: the emphasis on harmony and *li* might have been interpreted as a push to address even minor threats to China’s regional order. That said, the widespread belief that using force for such reasons is immoral makes this exception unlikely to be a major factor.  

**Person A:** Fascinating. How did historical events like the Opium Wars shape China’s sense of nationalism?  

**Person B:** Great question! The Opium Wars in 1839 marked the end of China’s long-held belief in its moral and cultural superiority. Until 1949, when modern China was founded, the country grappled with the "century of humiliation" caused by foreign powers and Japan. This trauma became a driving force for nationalism, which drew on Confucian traditions that had persisted for over 2,000 years. The narrative of reclaiming China’s "rightful place" as a global power emerged from this, even as China insisted it would pursue its goals peacefully, in line with Confucian principles.  

**Person A:** I’ve heard that Confucianism and political realism overlap in Chinese nationalism. How does that work?  

**Person B:** Oh, it’s quite a blend! Chinese nationalism isn’t just about cultural identity—it also incorporates realist ideas about power and geopolitics. Think of it as "realpolitik nationalism," where concepts like territorial integrity, sovereignty, and international legitimacy are framed as national imperatives. This fusion means that China’s strategic goals, such as achieving strategic parity or reshaping global rules to favor its rise, are tied to both Confucian ideals and realist calculations. It’s like a hybrid ideology that merges moral and material considerations.  

**Person A:** That’s a lot to unpack. How does this all tie back to China’s current strategic culture?  

**Person B:** Surveys suggest that China’s strategic culture is shaped by a mix of Confucian values and realist principles. The emphasis on national wealth and power, for example, aligns with both the Confucian model of governance and realist assumptions about state interests. This duality explains why many Chinese respondents’ strategic preferences don’t always match Confucian pacifism. Instead, they’re driven by a complex interplay of historical memory, cultural identity, and the need to assert China’s place in a rapidly changing world.  

**Person A:** Thanks for breaking that down—it’s really eye-opening!  

**Person B:** Of course! It’s amazing how deeply history and philosophy shape modern geopolitics. Let me know if you want to dive deeper into any of these points!  
Beliefs about cultural history and international image are likely shaped by Confucianism, which also forms major constitutive elements of Chinese nationalism, defined as emphasizing the primacy and virtues of shared ethnic, cultural, and political identity for a discrete population; the Sino-centric Confucian model of governance created an innate sense of entitlement and civilizational supremacy, with China defining "civilization" through enculturation of invaders and the tributary system, which affirmed the superiority and centrality of the "Central Kingdom" and established a bilateral system of international relations treating others as uncivilized barbarians, while the perception that the tribute system was economically ruinous for China further reinforced moral superiority; survey results did not indicate strong presence or influence of Confucian values like yi and ren, nor consistency with norms such as filial piety or reciprocity, even when facing varying threat severities or justified war scenarios, though harmony and li could be interpreted as incentivizing aggressive rectification of threats to a Chinese-led order, yet the widespread belief in the immorality of using force for such reasons made these exceptions unlikely to be a strong causal factor; nationalism's emotional commitment to the status quo and denigration of out-groups, alongside its role in ensuring domestic stability and unifying agendas, coexists with modern China's intense pride over international recognition, making Chinese nationalists prone to feeling slighted by perceived interference with sovereignty or legitimacy, rooted in the Confucian concept of fuqing (wealth and power), which demands proactive foreign policy to remove the stigma of a victim nation and establish an international order favorable to China's rise, as realpolitik nationalism—fusing political realism with Confucian ideals—clothes realist ideas of power politics in nationalist rhetoric, elevating territorial integrity and sovereignty to national imperatives, even as survey results suggest China's strategic culture blends Confucian and realist beliefs, emphasizing wealth and power as goals consistent with both models, despite nationalism's potential to deviate from realpolitik's rational pursuit of material interests, all while the Opium Wars in 1839 and the founding of modern China in 1949 marked the end of China's preeminence, leading to nationalism's emergence as an alternative to Confucian civilization, drawing on 2,000-year-old traditions to fuel an emotional imperative to reassume "Great Power" status, even as China insists on pursuing its goals peacefully and orderly, according to Confucian principles, while the integration of Westphalian norms into Chinese discourse underscores the fusion of realist aspirations with nationalist fervor, shaping strategic preferences that often diverge from Confucian pacifism.

204.atomic_facts:
1. The CPC Central Committee is mentioned in "People's Daily Online" on November 15, 2012.
2. James Mulvenon wrote "The King is Dead! Long Live the King! The CMC Leadership Transition from Jiang to Hu" in China Leadership Monitor on January 30, 2005.
3. Jiang Zemin was declared the "core" of the CPC by his predecessor Deng Xiaoping.
4. Jiang Zemin's designation as "core" was likely to promote stability after the 1989 Tiananmen Square massacre.
5. Hu Jintao was never granted the designation of "core."
6. Neil Thomas provided written testimony for U.S.-China Economic and Security Review Commission Hearing on CCP Decision-Making and the 20th Party Congress on January 27, 2022.
7. Chris Buckley reported that China's Communist Party declared Xi Jinping as "core" leader in the New York Times on October 27, 2016.
8. Alice Miller wrote " 'Core' Leaders, 'Authoritative Persons,' and Reform Pushback" in China Leadership Monitor on July 19, 2016.
9. Jiang Zemin's "Three Represents" was not incorporated into CCP documents until after his term as general secretary ended.
10. Hu Jintao's "Scientific Outlook on Development" was not incorporated into CCP documents until after his term as general secretary ended.
11. Xi's contribution to the CCP Charter is considered more politically significant because it includes his name and was formalized during his time in office.
12. The National People's Congress of the People's Republic of China explained the "Amendment to the Constitution of the People's Republic of China (Draft)" on March 20, 2018.
13. Reuters reported that China would enshrine Xi's Thought into the state constitution in January 19, 2018.
14. The 19th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party adopted the CCP Charter on October 24, 2017.

**Person A:** Hey, I’ve been reading about China’s political structure and noticed the term “core” leader comes up a lot. Can you explain how that designation works?  
**Person B:** Sure! The concept of a “core” leader in the Communist Party (CPC) has historical roots. For example, Jiang Zemin was declared the “core” by his predecessor, Deng Xiaoping, around the time of the 1989 Tiananmen Square events. Interestingly, Hu Jintao never received that title during his tenure.  

**Person A:** Wait, so the designation isn’t automatic? What makes it different for Xi Jinping?  
**Person B:** Exactly! Xi’s case is unique because his contribution to the Party’s ideology—specifically, the inclusion of his name in the CCP Charter—was formalized *while he was still in office*. That’s a shift from earlier leaders like Jiang Zemin, whose “Three Represents” theory wasn’t officially adopted until *after* his term as general secretary ended.  

**Person A:** Oh, I see. So the timing and naming matter a lot?  
**Person B:** Absolutely. Take Hu Jintao’s “Scientific Outlook on Development”—it wasn’t enshrined in Party documents until *after* his presidency. In contrast, Xi’s Thought was enshrined in the state constitution in 2018, as reported by Reuters, during a period of national “fervor” around the 19th National Congress.  

**Person A:** That’s fascinating. How did the Party decide to formalize Xi’s ideology so prominently?  
**Person B:** Well, the National People’s Congress explained the constitutional amendment in March 2018, emphasizing Xi’s role as a central figure. It’s a deliberate move to solidify his legacy, unlike earlier leaders whose contributions were only later recognized.  

**Person A:** Thanks for breaking that down! I guess the “core” designation is more than just a title—it’s a political strategy.  
**Person B:** Exactly. It’s all about continuity and authority. Deng Xiaoping’s influence in the 1990s set the precedent, and now Xi’s era is shaping the next chapter.  

**Person A:** I’ll have to read more about the 20th Party Congress hearings—Neil Thomas’s testimony mentioned something about decision-making.  
**Person B:** Oh, right! His 2022 testimony for the U.S.-China Economic and Security Review Commission touched on how the Party’s leadership transitions are managed. It’s a topic that’s still evolving.  

**Person A:** Thanks for the chat—I feel like I’ve got a clearer picture now!  
**Person B:** Anytime! China’s political dynamics are complex, but breaking it down fact by fact helps. Let me know if you need more details!  

- The 19th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party adopted the CCP Charter on October 24, 2017.  
- Deng Xiaoping's authority led to Jiang Zemin being designated as the "core" of the CPC, likely to stabilize the party after the 1989 Tiananmen Square massacre.  
- The National People's Congress of the People's Republic of China explained the "Amendment to the Constitution of the People's Republic of China (Draft)" on March 20, 2018.  
- Reuters reported on January 19, 2018, that China would enshrine Xi's Thought into the state constitution.  
- Hu Jintao was never granted the designation of "core," unlike Jiang Zemin, whose "Three Represents" was not incorporated into CCP documents until after his term as general secretary ended.  
- Alice Miller authored " 'Core' Leaders, 'Authoritative Persons,' and Reform Pushback" in China Leadership Monitor on July 19, 2016.  
- The formalization of Xi's contribution during his tenure, including his name, is viewed as more politically significant than Jiang Zemin's or Hu Jintao's policies.  
- Chris Buckley reported in the New York Times on October 27, 2016, that China's Communist Party declared Xi Jinping as "core" leader.  
- Jiang Zemin's "Three Represents" and Hu Jintao's "Scientific Outlook on Development" were not incorporated into CCP documents until after their respective terms as general secretary ended.
